summary: Stancil reflects on how a decade of startup ambition and workaholism strained his relationship with his parents, costing them time and attention he never gave back. With Mode’s sale, he pauses his usual arguments to offer a heartfelt apology and gratitude, acknowledging their sacrifices and affirming the personal meaning of what was built.
money_quotes: So I said it wouldn’t cost me anything. But I didn't say what it would cost you.
I lived my life one fiscal quarter at a time, convinced that things would calm down in three months.
There are only two people who always called, even when I didn’t answer.
Two hundred million dollars is not, here in Silicon Valley, all that much money; we obsess over billions here, not millions.
And it has made a very lasting impact on me.
key_insight: The true cost of startup success is often paid in neglected relationships, making gratitude and repair as important as the outcome.
